ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific shrimp XO fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried rice as a generic category. No specific XO sauce or shrimp variant code exists; closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for shrimp XO fried rice or fried rice dish as a food concept in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker or canteen serving of shrimp XO fried rice is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served in Singapore food courts and school canteens.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp XO Fried Rice — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Shrimp XO Fried Rice (cr_id F05100129). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central fried rice analogues as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, fried rice analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for shrimp XO fried rice. GI of 75 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based fried rice (GI ~72–80 range). A midpoint value of 75 is used, consistent with the input record. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 65.15) / 100 = 48.9, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 26.06 × 2.5 = 65.15 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 491 mg per 100 g; a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 1,228 mg sodium — over half the AHA recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg — driven by XO sauce, soy sauce, and dried seafood components.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g, request reduced XO sauce and soy sauce during preparation, and avoid adding condiments at the table to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~75) with very low dietary fibre (0.6 g per 100 g) produces a significant post-meal glucose spike; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 65 g of rapidly digestible carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 150 g or fewer, consider substituting white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower the glycaemic response, and add fibre-rich vegetables such as broccoli or bok choy to blunt the post-meal glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (491 mg per 100 g) and moderate fat (8.4 g per 100 g) from stir-frying oil and XO sauce raise cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is estimated at approximately 1.8 g per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict portion size to 150 g, request preparation with minimal oil and reduced XO sauce, and pair the dish with steamed vegetables to improve the overall dietary fat and sodium profile of the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 491 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold; XO sauce contains chilli (a nightshade) and dried seafood; high-GI white rice with negligible fibre promotes systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish, monitor personal response to nightshade ingredients (chilli in XO sauce), substitute white rice with brown rice, and request reduced XO sauce to lower both sodium and pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shrimp XO Fried Rice (虾仁XO酱炒饭) is a popular Singaporean Chinese stir-fried rice dish prepared by wok-frying cooked white rice with fresh or frozen shrimp, XO sauce, egg, spring onion, soy sauce, and cooking oil over high heat. XO sauce is a premium Hong Kong-origin condiment made from dried scallops, dried shrimp, Jinhua ham, chilli, garlic, and shallots, lending the dish a complex umami-rich, mildly spicy, and slightly smoky flavour profile. The texture is characterised by individual, slightly chewy rice grains coated in the aromatic sauce, with tender shrimp pieces throughout. Per 100 g, the dish provides 217 kcal, 9.2 g protein, 8.4 g fat, 26.1 g carbohydrate, 0.6 g dietary fibre, and 491 mg sodium. The high sodium content (491 mg per 100 g) driven by XO sauce and soy sauce makes this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The high-GI white rice base (GI ~75) with minimal fibre produces a rapid post-meal glucose rise, making portion control essential for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. A standard hawker serving of approximately 250 g delivers roughly 1,228 mg sodium and 65 g of carbohydrate. The dish is commonly found in Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and school canteens. It contains major allergens including crustaceans, shellfish, egg, soy, gluten, and fish-derived dried seafood in the XO sauce. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific preparation confirmation.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100129) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central fried rice analogues and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous white rice fried rice entries. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchors are defensible approximate matches.
notes: 1. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 per HPB source; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry and is plausible given the savoury preparation with no added sugar ingredients. 2. saturated_fat_g (1.8 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried rice with shrimp (NDB 57589 analogue) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific shrimp XO fried rice preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (75 × 65.15) / 100 = 48.9, based on a 250 g standard serving containing 65.15 g carbohydrate. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; XO sauce typically contains pork-derived Jinhua ham, making this dish non-halal in standard preparation. 6. is_halal is set to false based on standard XO sauce formulation containing pork-derived ingredients.
